content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
January 31,
1995
William L. McClure
Office of the City Attorney
1100 Alma St.,
Ste.
210
Menlo Park,
CA
94025
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-95-024
Dear Mr. McClure:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Menlo Park City Counciliuember Stephen Schmidt regarding
his responsibilities under the conflict—of-interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
QUESTION
May Councilmember Schiitidt participate in decisions affecting
the experimental traffic control program for the Willow’s
neighborhood of the City of Menlo Park, despite owning property
within 300 feet of a traffic control device established as part of
the project?
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No. A-95-024
Page
2
CONCLUSION
Councilmember Schmidt may not participate in decisions
affecting a traffic control device situated within 300 feet of his
home.
He may participate in other components of the program so
long as the decisions for which he has a disqualifying financial
interest are segregated from the other decisions, considered first
without the councilmember participating in any way,
and a final
decision reached by the city council.
If this is done, the
councilmember may participate in the deliberations regarding other
portions of the plan,
so long as those deliberations do not result
in a reopening or in any way affect the decisions from which he
was disqualified.
The councilmember may not participate in the
final adoption of the project.
FACTS
The City of Menlo Park has been conducting an experimental
traffic control program (the “program”) within the Willows
neighborhood of the city.
The program included the installation
of a number of traffic circles, stop signs, chokers,
and other
physical improvements to the neighborhood’s streets to slow
traffic in the residential neighborhood.
The program currently
includes 15 to 20 traffic control devices placed on 10 streets in
the neighborhood.
The program was initiated by the previous city council (not
including Councilmember Schmidt).
At the time the program was
adopted, the city council directed that it be reviewed again after
one year by the city council.
The city council will have the
option of terminating the program, modifying it, or making it
permanent.
Counciliuember Schmidt has since been elected to the Menlo
Park City Council.
Councilmeinber Schmidt owns and resides in a
home in the Willows area,
and within 300 feet of one of the
traffic circles installed as part of the program.
The Willows neighborhood consists of approximately 700 homes
with approximately 2,000 people.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise-usi-ng his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:


Our File No. A-95-024
Page
3
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
Section 871Ô3(b).
You have requested advice concerning potential conflicts of
interest resulting from Councilmember Schmidt’s interest in real
property, the ownership of his home.
We assume the councilmember
has an ownership interest in his home worth $1,000 or more.
Pursuant to Section 87103 (b), the counciliuember may not
participate in any decision that will have a foreseeable and
material financial effect on his real property.
Foreseeabilitv and Materiality
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
There is a substantial likelihood that reducing the traffic
flow on streets near the councilmember’s home will have some
financial effect on the value of the property.
(See e.g.,
Lee
Advice Letter,
No.
A—94-236.)
However, the foreseeable effect on the councilmember’s
economic interest must also be material to require
disqualification.
The Commission has adopted differing guidelines
to determine whether an effect is material, depending on the
specific circumstances of each decision.
The effect of a decision
on real property in which an official has a direct, indirect or
beneficial ownership interest, is material if the official’s
property is directly involved in a decision.
2
Under your facts,
the councilmember’s real property will not be directly affected by
the decision in question.
In all other cases, materiality is determined by applying an
indirect financial effect test.
To determine the materiality of
an indirect effect of a decision on property which is owned by an
of-f-i-cial,
one of three tests will generalLy be applied.
2
Regulation 18702.1(a) (3) provides a list of situations in which
an official’s property is directly involved in a decision.
Some
examples include decisions to zone or rezone the official’s
property, decisions to annex the property, decisions concerning
assessments on the property and decisions to include the property
in or exclude it from any city, county or other local governmental
subdivision.


Our File No.
A—95-024
Page
4
(1)
The effect of a decision is material if the official
owns property within 300 feet of property that is the subject of a
decision and the decision will have some financial effect on the
property.
(Regulation 18702.3(a) (1).)
For example, you stated that the traffic control program
included a traffic circle within 300 feet of the councilmember’s
home.
Pursuant to Regulation 18702.3(a) (1), if the decision will
have any financial effect on the value of the councilmember’s
property, the effect of the decision is deemed material.
3
(2)
If the decision involves construction of, or
improvements to, streets, water, sewer,
storm drainage or similar
facilities,
and the official’s property will receive new or
substantially improved services, the effect of the decision is
also deemed material.
(Regulation 18702.3(a) (2).)
(3)
Where the official’s real property is beyond a radius of
300 feet from the subject property, but within 2,500 feet, the
effect of a decision will be material only if the decision will
affect the value of the official’s property by $10,000 or more, or
the rental value of the property by $1,000 in a 12 month period.
4
(Regulation 18702.3(a) (3).)
The Act also provides a “public generally” exception which
applies where a governmental decision will affect a significant
segment of the population in substantially the same manner as the
decision will affect the interests of the official.
Where a
decision concerns the material financial effect caused by a
traffic circle within 300 feet of the councilmember’s residence,
it is unlikely that the decision will affect a significant segment
of Menlo Park in substantially the same manner.
‘
Regulation 18702.3(b)
also provides that where an official’s
property is located beyond a 2,500 foot radius of the subject
property, the effect of the decision is material only if there are
specific circumstances regarding the decision which make it
foreseeable that the decision will affect the value of the
official’s prbpèrty hy$IO,O00 or more, or the reitä1 vaIu Of th
property by $1,000 or more per 12 month period.
However,
even if
the decision has such an effect on the property, the effect is not
deemed material if the effect on the official’s property will be
substantially the same as the effect upon at least 25 percent of
all the properties within a 2,500 foot radius of the official’s
property,
and there are at least 10 properties under separate
ownership within a 2,500 foot radius of the official’s property.
(Regulation 18702.3 (b).)


Our File No.
A—95-024
Page 5
It appears from your facts that generally, many of the other
decisions will affect property beyond 300 feet, but within 2,500
feet of the councilmember’s property.
Under these circumstances
he may participate so long as the decision will not affect the
value of the property by $10,000 or more.
Secnnentation
Please note, if you determine that the councilmember will
have a conflict of interest with respect to decisions concerning
certain aspects of the traffic control program, the city
councilmember will also have to disqualify himself with respect to
all decisions that are interliniced to those decisions.
For
example, decisions concerning funding of the work are inseparable
from the actual execution of the program with respect to which the
official may have a conflict of interest.
(Sweeney Advice Letter,
No.
A—89—639.)
Moreover, if one decision is an alternative to a
decision for which the official has a conflict of interest, the
official will be disqualified from both the decisions.
(DuVivier
Advice Letter,
No.
1-92-363.)
Conversely,
in many cases, large projects may be divided into
separate decisions so that a public official with a disqualifying
interest as to one component may participate in considering other
components, provided, of course, that the official has no other
disqualifying interest with respect to the other components.
(Huffaker Advice Letter,
No.
A—86-343; Davis Advice Letter,
No.
A-
92-347.)
If this is the case, the councilmember may vote on other
traffic control devices under the following circumstances:
(1)
The decisions for which the councilmember has a
disqualifying financial interest should be segregated from the
other decisions.
(2)
The decisions from which the councilmember is
disqualified should be conidered first,
and a final decision
reached by the city council without the councilmember
participating in any way.
(3)
Once a decision has been made on the portions of the
program for which the councilmember has a disqualifying interest,
the councilmember may participate in the deliberations regarding
other portions of the plan,
so long as those deliberations do not
result in a reopening or in any way affect the decisions from
which he wasdisquaiifie&.
Please note that the councilmember may not participate in the
final vote on the program since the vote on the final program will


Our File No.
A—95—024
Page 6
necessarily affect the decision from which he was disqualified.
(Lanzone Advice Letter, No. A-93-135.)
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322-59O1.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchweli
Li Counsel
Q9
ohn W. Wallace
Legal Division
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 1Oc per page.

the experimental traffic control program for the Willow’s
neighborhood of the City of Menlo Park, despite owning property
within 300 feet of a traffic control device established as part of
the project?
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
 •
(916) 322-5660


Our Page
2

CONCLUSION: Councilmember Schmidt may not participate in decisions
affecting a traffic control device situated within 300 feet of his
home.
He may participate in other components of the program so
long as the decisions for which he has a disqualifying financial
interest are segregated from the other decisions, considered first

and a final
decision reached by the city council.

so long as those deliberations do not result

The councilmember may not participate in the
